title: A/B Testing
text:
Previously, we studied situations where the main interest is not the exact value of a population parameter but, instead, which of the two mutually exclusive possible scenarios, namely \(H_0\) or \(H_1\), is true. For example, we are not interested in the exact value of the population mean, \(\mu\), but we are interested in knowing if \(\mu\) is higher than \(35\)mm or not.
In a classical hypothesis test, we generally use \(H_0\) as the status quo hypothesis (i.e., the hypothesis of nochange, no difference), where \(H_1\) represents the anticipated change. Note that \(H_1\) is the alternative hypothesis, in the sense that if \(H_0\) is false, then \(H_1\) is true. It is not allowed for both hypotheses to be false; one of the two hypotheses must be true. Also, the hypotheses are mutually exclusive (i.e., both hypotheses cannot be true simultaneously).
The general procedure for hypothesis testing is always the same:

title: A/B Testing
text:
Motivational Problem
In 2008, Obama’s campaign was looking to increase the total amount of donations to the campaign. To donate online, people needed to subscribe to the campaign e-mail by clicking on a red button saying “Sign Up”.



Original website of the campaign.

Source: Optimizely



After a while, they started wondering: Is this website’s design effective? They decided to test it! The campaign created three alternative button designs (with text: SIGN UP NOW, LEARN MORE, JOIN US NOW) and five different media choices to replace the original photo: two alternative photos and three videos. In total, they compared 24 website designs. they measured the subscription rate of each design. As it turns out, the best design had over 40% higher subscription rate than the original website being used. It was estimated that the additional subscription generated an additional 60 million dollars worth of donations and 288,000 additional volunteers. You can learn more about this application of A/B testing here.

But how do we compare websites?


The response variable
The first step is to understand the purpose of the website. This is a fundamental step because it guides the creation of useful metrics of success. Defining the main purpose of a website is not always a simple task. For example, in their case:



Do they want the website to attract more subscribers?




Do they want a high proportion of visitors to become donors?




Do they want to increase the size of donation per visitor?



What is a good metric to measure how effective the website is? Such metric will be the response variable of the study. Although the campaign wanted to increase the total amount of donations, this was not the website’s purpose. The purpose of the website was to attract subscribers. For this reason, they used the rate of subscription, i.e., the number of people that subscribed divided by the number of people that visited the website. If the website’s purpose is not very well defined, you might (and probably will) come up with metrics that are misleading on how effective your website is.


The covariates
The second step is to identify the elements that could be optimized. In their case, they considered the media and the button. But they could consider other factors too, such as the background colour, for example. In essence, they are trying to find the configuration of the covariates, media and button, that would yield the highest subscriber rate.


Randomization
To avoid bias, each visitor saw a randomly chosen website design. This is a key step to be able to conclude that the reason for the increase in the subscription was the website’s design, and not a hidden factor that is not even being considered, lurking variable. The idea is that randomization will “average out” all these hidden differences between the visitors, and the only difference between the groups would be the website seen.

title: A/B Testing
text:
A/B Testing
A/B testing is not only about website optimization. In general, we have two populations (or groups), namely Group A and Group B (hence A/B), and we want to compare these two populations with respect to a variable of interest (response variable). Let’s see a few examples:
A new vaccine has been developed for cancer. The drug company wants to check the efficacy of the vaccine. The company randomly split 50,000 volunteers into two groups, where 25,000 will receive the vaccine (Group A) and 25,000 will receive the placebo (Group B). They measure if the individuals develop cancer in the next ten years.

Response variable (\(Y\)): whether the individuals develop cancer;
Covariate (\(X\)): whether the individuals receives the vaccine or the placebo (two-levels);
Parameters of interest: \(p_1\) and \(p_2\), the proportions of individuals who develop cancer in Group A and Group B, respectively;
Research question: is the vaccine effective? In other words, is \(p_1 < p_2\)?




 A phone company wants to reduce the number of complaints against its customer services. They are considering removing the navigation menu from the support service and using support staff instead. Naturally, this will be an expensive move, so they first want to test it to see if it would be effective. They trained a small team and randomly directed the clients to the navigation menu or human support. Then, they monitor whether the clients will open a complaint at the Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC).

Response variable (\(Y\)): whether the individual opens a complaint;
Covariate (\(X\)): whether the individual is directed to the navigation menu or the support staff (two-levels);
Parameters of interest: \(p_1\) and \(p_2\), the proportion of clients that open a complaint at CRTC in Group A and Group B, respectively;
Research question: is the support staff better? In other words, is \(p_1 < p_2\)?




 An e-commerce company wants to compare two website designs with respect to sales in dollars. For the following \(N\) clients, the design each client will see will be selected at random.

Response variable (\(Y\)): the amount of dollars spent;
Covariate (\(X\)): two website designs (two-levels);
Parameters of interest: \(\mu_1\) and \(\mu_2\), the average amount of dollars spent by the clients in each website design;
Research question: Is one of the designs better?





In general, the structure of A/B Testing consists of:

a response variable, \(Y\);
a covariate, \(X\), that splits the population into two groups;
randomization, individuals are randomly assigned to the groups; and
statistical comparison of the groups’ parameter of interest (remember that all we have is a sample, so we need to account for the sampling variability.




A/B Testing Workflow



How is Obama’s Campaign Problem Different?
Note that in case of the Obama’s campaign website, we have:

Response variable: the subscription rate; ✅
Randomization; ✅
One covariate that splits the population into two groups; ✘

We had two covariates, \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), which are the button design and the media used, respectively. Besides, \(X_1\) had four levels (i.e., possible values): the four design options or the button. This would split the population into four groups, not into two. \(X_2\), the media variable, had six levels, three photos and three videos. Combining \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) results in a total of 24 groups.
This problem is slightly more complex than having only two groups. If we performed pairwise comparisons, we would need 276 tests to compare all 24 groups. As we learned previously, this would considerably inflate the probability of error. For now, let us restrict our focus to only two groups. We will discuss more groups later.


Comparing the two groups
Once the appropriate response variable and covariate have been specified, we start collecting the data. The data collected will be only a sample of the population; therefore, we need to take into account the sampling variability. We are already familiar with the methodology to conduct this statistical analysis, namely two-samples confidence intervals and hypothesis tests. Let’s refresh our memory!
When estimating or testing hypotheses, the parameter of interest affects which statistic we are going to use. For example, when testing the mean, we want to use the sample mean \(\bar{X}\), when testing difference in proportion, we want to use the difference in sample proportions, \(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2\), and so on.
Naturally, the way these statistics behave are different, i.e., the sampling distributions (and null models) of these statistics are different. We have explored two main approaches to approximate the sampling distribution (for confidence intervals) and the null model (for hypothesis testing): (1) the Central Limit Theorem (CLT); and (2) Simulation Based Approaches.

Central Limit Theorem
When estimating or testing hypotheses, the parameter of interest affects which statistic we are going to use. For example, when testing the mean, we want to use \(\bar{X}\), whereas when testing the median, we want to use the sample median, and so on. The Central Limit Theorem provides an approximation for the distribution of certain test statistics for large sample size.

Comparing two means
Suppose you want to test the difference between two independent populations’ means. The scenarios to be considered:

\(H_0: \mu_A - \mu_B = d_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu_A - \mu_B \neq d_0\)
\(H_0: \mu_A - \mu_B = d_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu_A - \mu_B > d_0\)
\(H_0: \mu_A - \mu_B = d_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu_A - \mu_B < d_0\)

To conduct this hypothesis test, we take two independent samples, one from each population. By independent samples, we meant that the individuals are selected independently from each population.
Suppose Group A has \(n_A\) elements drawn at random from Population A, and Group B has \(n_B\) elements drawn at random from Population B. Let’s use \(x\) to refer to Group A and \(y\) to refer to Group B. For large samples sizes, the test statistic given by \[
T = \frac{\bar{x}-\bar{y} - d_0}{\sqrt{\frac{s_A^2}{n_A} - \frac{s_B^2}{n_B}} }
\] follows a \(t\)-distribution with approximately \(\nu\) degrees of freedom under \(H_0\), where \[
\nu = \frac{
\left(\frac{s_A^2}{n_A}+\frac{s_B^2}{n_B}\right)^2
}
{
\frac{s_A^4}{n_A^2(n_A-1)}+\frac{s_2^4}{n_B^2(n_B-1)}
}.
\]

In other words, the null model of the test statistic above is \(t_\nu\). Of course, we are never going to calculate this weird formula by hand! Our computers can do this for us.

 Suppose Obama’s campaign wanted to test which of two websites, Website A or Website B, results in a larger amount of donations. The next 60 users who visit the campaign’s website will access one of the websites chosen at random until 30 users have seen one of the designs. We have collected (actually simulated!) this data for you and stored it in the sample_money_donated object.

suppressMessages(library(tidyverse))
set.seed(1)

# Simulating a sample of 30 individuals for group A and Group B
sample_money_donated <- 
tibble(group = c("Website A", "Website B"), 
    amount = list(
        if_else(runif(30) < 0.5, 0, rnorm(30, 80, 10) ), 
        if_else(runif(30) < 0.6, 0, rnorm(30, 100, 20) ))
    ) %>% 
unnest(cols = amount) %>%
sample_n(60)

sample_money_donated

# A tibble: 60 × 2
   group     amount
   <chr>      <dbl>
 1 Website B    0  
 2 Website B    0  
 3 Website B    0  
 4 Website A    0  
 5 Website A   87.8
 6 Website B    0  
 7 Website A   66.2
 8 Website A    0  
 9 Website B   88.6
10 Website A    0  
# … with 50 more rows


Next, to test \(H_0: \mu_A - \mu_B = d_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu_A - \mu_B \neq d_0\), we can use the t.test function in R.

t.test(amount ~ group,  # The formula: "website affects amount".
       mu = 0, # the value of d0
       alternative = "two.sided", # "less" for < and "greater" for >;
       data = sample_money_donated)


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  amount by group
t = -0.85694, df = 54.453, p-value = 0.3952
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Website A and group Website B is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -35.02343  14.04580
sample estimates:
mean in group Website A mean in group Website B 
               37.16292                47.65173 




Comparing two proportions
Obama’s campaign wanted the website to increase the number of subscribers and, for this reason, they used the rate of subscription. In this case, the variable of interest, “whether a visitor subscribes”, is not numerical, is dichotomic: “yes” or “no”. Consequently, we would want to compare the proportions of visitors who subscribes using Website A and Website B.
To test for the equality of proportions between two groups, i.e., \(H_0: p_A - p_B = 0\) vs \(H_1: p_A - p_B \neq 0\), we can use the following test statistics: \[
Z = \frac{\hat{p}_A-\hat{p}_B}{\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\left(\frac{1}{n_A}+\frac{1}{n_B}\right)}},
\]

where \(\hat{p}=\frac{n_A\hat{p}_A+n_B\hat{p}_B}{n_A+n_B}\) is the overall proportion. For large sample sizes, the null model of the \(Z\) statistic is the Standard Normal distribution, \(N(0,1)\). Again, we will not do this manually, as we can easily calculate using the computer.

 Suppose Obama’s campaign wanted to test which of two websites, Website A or Website B, results in a higher rate of subscribers. The next 60 users who visit the campaign’s website will access one of the websites chosen at random until 30 users have seen one of the designs. We have collected this data for you and stored it in the sample_subscriber object.

library(tidyverse)
library(infer)

Warning: package 'infer' was built under R version 4.2.3

set.seed(1)

# Simulating a sample of 30 individuals for group A and Group B
sample_subscriber <- 
tibble(website = factor(c("A", "B")), 
    subscribed = list(
        sample(factor(c("yes", "no")), 30, replace = TRUE, p=c(0.42, 0.58)), 
        sample(factor(c("yes", "no")), 30, replace = TRUE, p=c(0.37, 0.58)))
    ) %>% 
unnest(cols = subscribed) %>%
sample_n(60)

sample_subscriber

# A tibble: 60 × 2
   website subscribed
   <fct>   <fct>     
 1 B       no        
 2 B       yes       
 3 A       yes       
 4 A       no        
 5 B       no        
 6 A       no        
 7 A       no        
 8 B       no        
 9 A       yes       
10 A       no        
# … with 50 more rows


Once the data is collected, we can run prop.test in R to test the hypothesis.

prop.test(
    sample_subscriber %>% 
    group_by(website) %>% 
    summarise(n_successes = sum(subscribed == "yes"), 
            n_failures = sum(subscribed == "no")) %>% 
    select(-website) %>% 
    as.matrix())


    2-sample test for equality of proportions with continuity correction

data:  sample_subscriber %>% group_by(website) %>% summarise(n_successes = sum(subscribed == "yes"), n_failures = sum(subscribed == "no")) %>% select(-website) %>% as.matrix()
X-squared = 1.4459e-30, df = 1, p-value = 1
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.2493486  0.3160153
sample estimates:
   prop 1    prop 2 
0.4333333 0.4000000 





Simulation Based Approach
Alternatively to the CLT, we could use computer simulations to compare the groups. The same idea we discussed previously:

Collect the samples of Group A and Group B;
Calculate the observed test statistic;
Put the sample of both groups together;
Select \(n_1\) elements at random, without replacement, from the two samples combined.
The selected elements will be your “new” Group A, and the remaining will be your “new” Group B.
Calculate the appropriate test statistic (e.g., the difference of sample proportions or of sample means).
Perform Steps 4, 5 and 6, multiple times to obtain a sample from the Null Distribution .
Compare the observed test statistic with the simulated null model.

Note that by randomizing the groups in the sample (Steps 3, 4, and 5), we are trying to remove the difference between the groups, except for those differences due to sampling variability. But why are we doing that again? Because we want to simulate a sample from the Null Model, which is the model under the assumption that there is no difference between the groups.
 Let’s return to Obama’s campaign problem in the example above. We want to test which website, Website A or Website B, has a higher subscription rate. The data is stored in sample_subscriber.

sample_subscriber

# A tibble: 60 × 2
   website subscribed
   <fct>   <fct>     
 1 B       no        
 2 B       yes       
 3 A       yes       
 4 A       no        
 5 B       no        
 6 A       no        
 7 A       no        
 8 B       no        
 9 A       yes       
10 A       no        
# … with 50 more rows


First, we calculate the observed test statistic. Since we are comparing the proportions, we can use the difference of sample proportions as our test statistic.

obs_test_statistic <- 
    sample_subscriber %>% 
    specify(formula = subscribed ~ website, success = "yes") %>% 
    calculate(stat = "diff in props", order = c("A", "B"))

obs_test_statistic

Response: subscribed (factor)
Explanatory: website (factor)
# A tibble: 1 × 1
    stat
   <dbl>
1 0.0333


Next, we simulate a sample from the null model by following Steps 3-6. Luckily for us, we don’t need to do this manually as we are already familiar with infer’s workflow.

sample_null_model <- 
    sample_subscriber %>% 
    specify(formula = subscribed ~ website, success = "yes") %>% 
    hypothesise(null = "independence") %>% 
    generate(reps = 5000, type = "permute") %>% 
    calculate(stat = "diff in props", order = c("A", "B"))

sample_null_model

Response: subscribed (factor)
Explanatory: website (factor)
Null Hypothesis: independence
# A tibble: 5,000 × 2
   replicate    stat
       <int>   <dbl>
 1         1  0.1   
 2         2 -0.1   
 3         3 -0.0333
 4         4 -0.233 
 5         5 -0.0333
 6         6 -0.0333
 7         7  0.0333
 8         8  0.167 
 9         9 -0.1   
10        10  0.233 
# … with 4,990 more rows


Finally, we compare the observed test statistic with the null model. Since the observed test statistic is \(0.0333\), we calculate the proportion of values generated from the null model that are above \(0.0333\) or below \(-0.0333\). This will be the estimated p-value of the test.

sample_null_model %>% 
    get_p_value(obs_test_statistic, direction = "both")

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  p_value
    <dbl>
1   0.998





There’s nothing new in these analyses, right? We have already discussed statistical inference involving two-sample. So, why are we discussing this again? As it turns out, A/B Testing is just another name that was coined in the industry for Randomized Experiments, a well-established statistical methodology for experimentation. Hence the familiarity of the methods above. That would be the end of it, except that some challenges arise in practice that requires close attention.



Early Stopping
In many situations, there is crushing evidence that a group is performing better than the other. So, should a company keep spending resources and time to continue the experiment as planned? Continuing with the experiment could be unnecessarily costly.

 Suppose Pfizer, a pharmaceutical company, is conducting a clinical trial to test the effectiveness of a new treatment. They planned to have 1,000 participants in total, 500 of which will receive the new drug, and the remaining 500 will receive the placebo. However, at the current point in time, they have data of 600 participants, where 300 received the drug and 300 received the placebo. Among the 300 who received the drug, nobody died. On the other hand, among the 300 who received the placebo, 200 died. Should the FDA (Food and Drug Administration) still wait for the result of the remaining 400 participants? Or should they stop the clinical trial early and start distributing the medicine to people in need?




In general, we not only want to compare Groups A and B, but we also want to reach a conclusion as soon as possible. To do so, one would have to “peek” at the partially collected data to conduct the proper statistical analysis. However, when should we peek at the partially collected data?
A reasonable answer could be to monitor the data online. In other words, for every new observation in each group we have, we take a peek at the data.
Suppose Obama’s campaign wanted to test two website designs, and they are interested in the amount of money donated per visitor. So, they decided to conduct an A/B Test and start monitoring as soon as they had ten visitors for each design. Then, they would peek at the data again once both designs had received another visitor, and so on. If they detect a difference, they will immediately stop the experiment and make the best website version available to all visitors. They will allow a maximum number of visitors of 1,000 per design.



A/B Test with two versions of Obama’s campaign website.

Source: Obama’s picturesOptimizely






Multiple checks of p-value for different sample sizes





In the example above, Obama’s campaign wants to use a strategy that does not specify the sample size. So far, we have been discussing inference scenarios where the sample size is determined prior to the study. But in this case, the methodology will not change because the sample size will be fixed at each peek. The problem is that we are experimenting multiple times with different sample sizes.




When conducting hypothesis testing, what are the effects that sample size has on:




Probability of Type I Error






Probability of Type II Error






Power of the test





Show answers


Solution. 


Probability of Type I Error


None. Remember that the probability of Type I Error is the significance level, which is specified by us before the test is conducted.


Probability of Type II Error


The Probability of Type II Error decreases as the sample size increases. The reason is that both the null model and the sampling distribution of the test statistic will become narrower, hence reducing their overlap.


Power of the test


The power of the test is just one minus the probability of Type II Error. Since the probability of Type II Error decreases as the sample size increases, the power of the test increases as the sample size increases.




Ok, so Obama’s campaign seems to have a good plan. The probability of Type I Error at each peek will be the same; the power of the test will be smaller, but if it is already big enough to detect a difference, kudos! No downside here, right? Wrong!While the probability of Type I Error is the same at each peek, they only need to reject the hypothesis once in multiple tests.




When conducting multiple hypothesis testing, what happens to the family-wise errors?


 Show answers

Solution. Since you need to make the right decision multiple times, and each time there is a chance that you make the wrong decision, when you consider whether all the decisions you made are right, there is a much lower chance of that happening compared to each hypothesis testing individually.


 Let’s continue our previous example of Obama’s campaign. The first thing we should do is to test if the plan is good. But how can we do that if we do not know the truth (i.e., if there’s actually a difference between the two websites): Here’s our we are going to do it:


Let’s run the experiment 100 times.



One hundred A/B Tests with two versions of Obama’s campaign website.

Source: Obama’s pictures Optimizely





But this time, we are going to use the same website for both groups. This way, we know the truth – there’s no difference!!



One hundred A/B Test with the same version of Obama’s campaign website.

Source: Obama’s pictures Optimizely






Multiple checks of p-value for different sample sizes in each of the 100 experiments.




Let’s see in how many of the 100 experiments the null hypothesis is rejected at least once. That will estimate how bad the chance of our family-wise Type I Error will be.


In this case, using a significance level of \(5\%\), approximately \(22\%\) of the \(100\) A/B tests would have wrongly rejected \(H_0\) and concluded that the same version of the website is different from itself. This is way above our specified significance level of \(5\%\). Terrible, isn’t it? Obama’s campaign is in a bit of a pickle now. It seems they have to wait for 2000 people to visit the website before they can examine whether there’s a difference. Do they, though?
We have learned about p-value adjustments to control for Type I Error. Remember Bonferroni’s correction? So we could propose a compromise to them:
- “Hey, don’t peek at the data for every two people that visit your website. Instead, pick a reasonable number of tests, and I’ll adjust the p-value so we can control the probability of Type I Error.”

They can decide when to conduct the test, say for example, \(n = 50\), \(n = 100\), \(n = 250\), \(n = 500\), and \(n = 1000\). Doing this in the Obama’s problem would reduce the number of rejection to 7 experiment, much closer to the \(5\%\) specified. This approach is called principled peeking.

title: Sampling Distribution
text:
In 2020, there was a presidential election in the United States. Every election year, we see many surveys trying to draw a picture of how people will vote. But how do they do these surveys? Do these polls check how all the people are going to vote? According to this table from US Census Bureau there are approximately 233.5 million voting-age citizens. It would certainly require a lot of resources to ask all 233.5 million voting-age American citizens (in fact, this is what the election period is for, right? and we all know how complicated and expensive is organizing the election).
To make things more complicated, the political scenario changes quite fast. Therefore, time is also an issue. Even if you had the resources to ask everyone, many political events that swing voters’ opinions could happen during your survey, and by the time you are finished, your results are already outdated.
So, how can we learn (at least approximately) how voters are going to vote without asking every voter?
A natural answer for this question is: let us ask just a small portion of the voters how they are going to vote. This approach does provide us with a solution, but also many problems:
In this chapter, we are going to focus on points 3 and 4. We will discuss points 1 and 2 in more detail later. Let’s formalize some intuitive concepts.

title: Sampling Distribution
text:
Population and parameters

Target Population
The group of all items/people that we are interested in.

In general, we are interested in one or more unknown summary quantities that describe the entire population (as opposed to specific individuals in the population), and they are called parameters.

Parameter
A summary quantity of the population that is (usually) unknown.

Parameters are unknown, we are trying to learn them because they tell us information about the population. Let’s see an example:
 In the election example above, the group of all American voters is our population. Note that the population is not the group of all Americans. It is the group of all American voters. We are interested in the proportion of voters that will vote for each candidate, and the proportion of voters that will abstain from voting. In this case, we have more than one parameter of interest.

Caution
A common mistake many students make is to mix up a variable of interest with a parameter of interest. For example, one might be interested in the average dolphin weight, which is a parameter; the average weight is a quantity of the population, not of a single dolphin. However, the dolphin weight is what is being measured, i.e., the variable of interest.





Apple buys touchscreens from Samsung. Suppose that Apple only accepts a batch of screens if the screens’ average lifespan in the batch is above 24 months. Naturally, Apple cannot test all the screens in the batch; otherwise, there will be nothing left for them to install on their phones.




What is the population?






What is the variable of interest?






Say that Apple also wants to make sure that the life time of the screens are approximately the same for all the screens. In other words, Apple doesn’t want some phones having screen problems in 24 months and others in 48 months. What would be a populational parameter of interest in this case?





Show answers


Solution. 


What is the population?


In this case, the population is the batch of screens. Be careful, as the population is not all the screens produced by Samsung.


What is the variable of interest?


The lifespan of the screens.


Say that Apple also wants to make sure that the lifetime of the screens is approximately the same for all the screens. In other words, Apple doesn’t want some phones having screen problems in 24 months and others in 48 months. What would be a populational parameter of interest in this case?


The variance (or standard deviation) of the lifespan of the screens could be used to capture such information.







In times of Covid-19, the Canadian government started providing some aid to Canadian workers that lost their jobs. The provincial government of British Columbia (BC) is studying if it should also provide assistance to university students. To make the decision, BC’s government wants to know the average income of university students in the province.



What is the population?






What is the variable of interest?






Say that BC’s government is also interested on the inequality of students’ income. What would be the populational parameter in this case?





Show answers


Solution. 


What is the population?


All the university students in British Columbia.


What is the variable of interest?


The average income of university students in BC.


Say that BC’s government is also interested on the inequality of students’ income. What would be the populational parameter in this case?


The variance (or standard deviation) of BC’s university students.




If we could measure the variable of interest over the entire population (i.e., each individual of the population), we would obtain the Population Distribution.

Population Distribution
The population distribution is the distribution of the variable of interest over the entire population.

In general, the population distribution is unknown because we cannot measure the variable of interest over the entire population. In these cases, we draw a sample from the population to learn about the population distribution.

title: Sampling Distribution
text:
Sample
We are going to call sample any subset (sub-group of elements) of the population. What we hope for is that this subset would represent the population well, but this is not always the case. Even if the population is poorly represented by a sub-group of elements, such sub-group is still called a sample of the population.

Sample
A subset of the population.

It is like when you are cooking. Imagine you’re making a soup and want to know if the soup has enough salt. Then, we usually just taste a spoon of the soup (a sample). If that portion that you tasted lacks salt, you conclude that the whole soup lacks salt. This process is the so-called inference; you are extrapolating results from a sample to the entire population. Now imagine you’re cooking less waterly food, say, french fries. Then, you take one piece to see if you have put enough salt. But, just by chance, you ended with a piece that got too much salt on top of it, and you conclude wrongly that the whole basket of french fries is salty. In this case, you got a sample that doesn’t represent the population well. But again, it’s still a sample.

 


Soup Image from: https://melhorcomsaude.com.br/receita-de-sopa-light-de-repolho/
Fries image from: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/science/can-too-much-salt-lead-to-bad-skin

There are many different strategies to draw a sample. One of the most common strategy is called random sampling without replacement. To take a random sampling without replacement of size n elements from a population you must:



List all the elements of the population.




Select one element at random from the population (all elements must have the same probability of being selected).




Save the selected element and remove it from the population.




Repeat Steps 2. and 3. n times.



Although random sampling without replacement is one of the simplest ways to draw a sample, it is still quite effective. However, in some cases, it is not the best strategy you could use. But let’s not worry about this now.
Now that you know the concept of sample, we need to revisit the concept of population. As it was mentioned, a sample is a subset of the population. But frequently the population that the sample is drawn from is not the same population we are interested in. Let us see an example.
 In times of Covid-19, the Canadian government started providing some aid to Canadian workers that lost their jobs. The provincial government of British Columbia (BC) is studying if it should also provide some aid to university students. To make the decision, BC’s government wants to know the average income of university students in the province. There are many universities in BC, and sampling students from all universities can be costly and time-consuming. The government decides to sample students only from the University of British Columbia (UBC). By doing this, the sample that the government will be taking, comes from the population of all UBC students, which is a more restricted group than the group of all university students in BC.



Example 2.1 shows us that we need to improve our definition of population and make a distinction between the two populations.

Sampled Population
The group of all items/people which we are taking a sample from.

Ideally, the Sampled Population will coincide with the Target Population. If not, be aware that the sample results can only be extrapolated (by means of statistical inference) to the Sampled Population. To extrapolate from the Sampled Population to the Target Population, you need a justification beyond statistical inference – which could be valid but could be misleading and wrong. Careful!
It is also worth mentioning that in some cases the target population and sample population differ for reasons other than convenience. This is very common in medical research (see the exercise below).




Say that a Canadian pharmaceutical company created a drug for a new disease. But now, they need to test if the drug works on human beings. The company will administer the drug and a placebo on some people infected by the disease who are located Canada and compare the results to see if the drug works.




What is the target population?






What is the sampled population?






What should the company do to match the sampled population to the target population? Is this reasonable?





Show answers


Solution. 


What is the target population?


The target population would be all the people in the world.


What is the sampled population?


People that are already sick with the disease in Canada.


What should the company do to match the sampled population to the target population? Is this reasonable?


The company would need to be able to infect anyone in the world (not only Canada), so it could administer the drug. This is clearly not reasonable. We cannot allow a company to go out there infecting people with a disease to see if a treatment works! In this case, ethical issues prevent us from matching the sample population with the target population.





Sample Distribution
The sample distribution is the distribution of the variable of interest over a given sample.

 Let’s return to the STAT 100 grades example. But this time, we are going to take a random sample of 15 students to estimate the median grade.

Draw a random sample





Sample distribution of STAT 100 grades of 15 students.






Based on the Example 2.2, answer true or false:




Each time we take a random sample we will have a different sample distribution.





Each time we take a random sample we will have a different population distribution.





Show answers


Solution. 


Each time we take a random sample we will have a different sample distribution.


True!! Note that the sample distribution is the distribution of the sample you obtained! Since we sample at random, the sample distribution will change every time.


Each time we take a random sample we will have a different population distribution.


False!! Note that the sample is random, but the population is not. The population is fixed, with all the same individuals. Therefore, the population distribution is fixed.





Great! We now know some of the fundamental concepts we need. Let’s review the big picture.

title: Sampling Distribution
text:
Revisiting the Big Picture


We are interested in a specific quantity (parameter) of a given population of objects/people. For example, the average income of all UBC students.





Due to limited resources we cannot survey the whole population to obtain the exact value of the parameter;


We then take a sample at random from the population.





Next, we use the sample to learn about the parameter of the population. For example, if we are interested in the average income of a given population, we calculate the average income in the sample. The function/formula you are using is called estimator (e.g., sample mean, sample variance, sample quantile) and the value you obtain in a given sample is called estimate of the parameter.





Great, we are done! But wait for a second; the sample is random. The fact that we are using Chen, Mary, and Amy’s average income is just due chance. It could have been Ronald, Mary, and Lien, or Jean, Wang, and Adebola, or any other three UBC students. Different samples would give different results.



You might even end up with a sample that is not representative of the population. For example, imagine you select three unemployed students. The average income in your sample would be smaller than the average income of all UBC’s students, since not all UBC’s students are unemployed.
How can we rely on an approach if every time we take a sample, we get a different estimate?
Admittedly, since the estimate is a random value, the chances are that we will not hit the mark and get an estimate that exactly matches the value of the parameter of interest. However, we don’t need to. We just need a method that gives us estimates that are “close enough most of the time”.

Distribution of income of all residents of the city (population distribution).





Which of the following elements will affect the sampling distribution (keeping everything else constant)? Select all that apply:

   Population distribution   Sample distribution   Sample size   Estimator 


Show answers


Solution. 


Population distribution


True! The population distribution will certainly affect the sampling distribution. For example, if you have more variability in the population, you will most probably have more variability in the estimates as well.


Sample distribution


False! The sample distribution changes according to the sample obtained, and consequently the estimate. But the sampling distribution is not about one sample, it is about all possible samples.


Sample size


True! As we increase the sample size, the estimator tends to vary less from sample to sample, which would make its sampling distribution narrower.


Estimator


True! The sampling distribution is the distribution of a given estimator. If you change the estimator, you are also changing the sampling distribution. For example, imagine that you want to estimate the weight of the lightest fish in the aquarium. So, instead of the sample mean, you would calculate the sample minimum. The values in the table shown in Example 4.1 would be completely different, and consequently, so would be the sampling distribution.




Next, we will discuss two ways to approximate the sampling distribution from a single sample: (1) Bootstrapping; and (2) the Central Limit Theorem.

text:
Prostate Cancer Data
Let us take a problem-driven approach to introduce LASSO. Let’s start by loading some packages we will use.

# Loading some libraries
library(tidyverse) 
library(tidymodels)
library(knitr)
library(broom)
library(leaps)
library(glmnet)
library(Brq) # This package contains the data

We will use a dataset of 97 patients with prostate cancer (Stamey et al. 1989). The dataset is available in the Brq R package. Before starting the analysis, we should understand what data we have. The following are the variables in our data frame:

lcavol: log(cancer volume)
lweight: log(prostate weight)
age: age of the patient
lbph: log(amount of benign prostatic hyperplasia)
svi: Seminal vesicle invasion. (True or False)
lcp: log(capsular penetration)
gleason: Gleason score. The Gleason score measures how abnormal the tissue looks. The lower the score is, the more the cells look like regular prostate tissue. You can learn more about the Gleason Score here
pgg45: Percentage Gleason scores 4 or 5. Scores 4 and 5 are considered very high; the cells barely look like normal prostate tissue.
lpsa: log(prostate-specific antigen)

Unfortunately, the units for the variables are not easily available and are unknown. However, keep in mind that the units are fundamental to interpreting the model. So always make sure you know what the units are (even though I’m not sure what the units are in this case 😉).
Let’s load the data and take a look at the first few rows.

# Loading the data
data("Prostate")
cancer_prostate <- tibble(Prostate)

# Printing the first 10 rows
kable(head(cancer_prostate, 10), digits = 4)

 The first thing we need to do is to split the data into a training and a test set. We will use the training set to fit the model, including variable selection and the test set to evaluate the model’s performance. So let’s randomly split our data into 70% training and 30% test sets.

set.seed(1) # Let's set the seed for reproducibility

cancer_split <- initial_split(cancer_prostate, prop = 0.7)
cancer_train <- training(cancer_split)
cancer_test <- testing(cancer_split)

Now that we have our training set, we can use it how we want, as much as we want. Let’s start simple and look at some summary statistics of our variables. We will remove svi because it is a dichotomic variable (true or false) with a proportion of true of 0.209.

We want to model lpsa, the log(prostate-specific antigen), using the remaining variables as explanatory variables (or independent variables, or predictors, or features, or covariates – so many names for the same things, I know!). There are a wide variety of mathematical models we could use, linear Regression being one of the simplest ones. However, Linear Regression has the advantage of being highly interpretable while performing very well in many prediction tasks.

title: LASSO
text:
Linear Regression
In regression, we want to model the relationship between a numerical variable Y and some, say p, explanatory variables X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p. The linear regression equation is given by:

Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1X_{1i} + \beta_2X_{2i} + \ldots + \beta_3X_{pi} + \varepsilon_i,\quad i=1,\ldots,n
 where \varepsilon is random error.
In the Cancer Prostate case, our linear regression model is given by:
\begin{align*}
\underbrace{lpsa_i}_\text{$Y_i$} = &\ \beta_0 + \beta_1\times \underbrace{lcavol_i}_\text{$X_{i1}$} + \beta_2\times \underbrace{lweight_i}_\text{$X_{i2}$} + \beta_3\times \underbrace{age_i}_\text{$X_{i3}$} + \beta_4\times \underbrace{lbph_i}_\text{$X_{i4}$}\ + \\
& + \beta_5\times \underbrace{svi_i}_\text{$X_{i5}$} + \beta_6\times \underbrace{lcp_i}_\text{$X_{i6}$} + \beta_7\times  \underbrace{gleason_i}_\text{$X_{i7}$} + \beta_8\times \underbrace{pgg45_i}_\text{$X_{i8}$} + \varepsilon_i
\end{align*}
To fit this model to the data at hand, we find the values for the coefficients \bm{\beta} = \left(\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p\right) that minimizes the residual sum of squares (RSS), i.e.,

    RSS(\bm{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1X_{i1} - \ldots - \beta_p X_{ip}\right)^2


# Fitting the linear regression model
cancer_lm <- lm(lpsa ~ ., data = cancer_train)

# Printing the coefficients' information

So the values in the estimate columns are the values that minimize RSS for our training data of the Prostate Cancer dataset. If you are not very familiar with statistical inference, you can ignore the other columns.

Improving the Ordinary Least Square (OLS)
Basically, there are two reasons to fit a linear regression model:

Prediction: We want to predict the response based on the values of the predictors the best we can. So, we want our model to generalize well to unseen data.
Inference: We are interested in interpreting the association between our response and the predictors and checking their significance.

For prediction, we want to avoid overfitting to training the data. Remember, overfitting occurs when your model does very well in the training data but poorly in unseen data. A common approach to avoid overfitting is the so-called Regularization. Regularization means we restrict (or penalize) our optimization a little bit by adding a penalty term to our cost function.
For inference, we should use the simplest model possible. What do we mean by the simplest model here? The model with the fewest predictors. There are mainly two reasons for this: (1) it is easier to interpret a model with fewer covariates; and (2) the principle of parsimony (Occam’s razor). As you can imagine, selecting only the relevant covariates for our model is vital in statistical modelling.
We shall now discuss both aspects: (1) variable selection and (2) regularization. As it turns out, the LASSO method we will discuss next is a great tool for both purposes.

Variable Selection
The easiest way (intuitively, at least) to improve our model is to try the model with all possible combinations of covariates, and then we select the best combination for our final model. Simple, eh? Yeah, the problem is that, even for a relatively small number of covariates, it quickly becomes impossible to check all possible models. The table below shows the number of models we need to try for different numbers of covariates.

Can you imagine fitting over 1 trillion models to find the best subset of covariates to use? Because of this, people have developed some strategies, such as Backward Selection, Forward Selection.
In Backward Selection, we first consider all the covariates. Then, we look for the least important covariate and remove it from the model. We do this iteratively until we have the best model. The Forward Selection works in a similar fashion, but we start with the null model (no covariates) and add one covariate at a time. There are mainly two problems with using Backward or Forward selection.
The first problem is that these methods are not guaranteed to find the best possible subset of covariates. Instead of trying 1,099,511,627,775 for 40 covariates, we will try 820 models with Backward or Forward selection. Much better, but plenty of possible combinations of covariates are not being considered.
The second problem is that this is not a smooth process. We either include a covariable or not. There is no “partially” including a variable. This lack of smoothness makes the results of these methods quite sensitive to small numerical changes in the data. Then it comes regularization.


Regularization
As we know, to fit a linear regression we look for the coefficients \bm{\beta} that minimizes the Residual Sum of Square (RSS). So any \bm{\beta} is fair game. We are only interested in driving our RSS down.
The idea of regularization is to restrict our search a bit by imposing a cap on our \bm{\beta}. So basically, we are saying:

ok, I’m looking to minimize the RSS, but I don’t want my \bm{\beta} to get too crazy (in other words, very high or very low values).

But how do we do this? We will add a penalty term to our RSS to tell our model that there is a price to be paid to use a high value (positive or negative) for \bm{\beta}:

\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n \left(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1X_{i1} - \ldots - \beta_p X_{ip}\right)^2}_\text{RSS} + {\color{red} \text{penalty term}}

There are many penalties we can add to our RSS. Two widely used ones are:

\lambda\sum_{i=1}^p \beta_i ^2 this leads to the so-called Ridge Regression.
\lambda\sum_{i=1}^p \left|\beta_i\right| this leads to the so-called LASSO.

Important: Note that the we do not include the intercept \beta_0.
The penalty term has two components a positive constant \lambda and a norm of \bm{\beta}. It is like when you go to the grocery store to buy potatoes. The constant \lambda is the price of the potatoes, and the norm (either \sum_{i=1}^p \beta_i ^2 or \sum_{i=1}^p \left|\beta_i\right|) measures the amount of potatoes you are buying. The different norms refer to different ways to measure the amount of potatoes being bought. For example, the grocery store could charge you per pound of potatoes, or it could charge you per unit of potatoes.
Naturally, the higher the price of the potato, the fewer potatoes you will buy. The same happens with the constant \lambda. The higher \lambda is, the smaller the values for \bm{\beta} we will use. Note that increasing the magnitude of any \beta_j will increase the penalty term, and it will only be acceptable if there is a significant decrease in the RSS to compensate for the increase in the penalty term. Because of the forced reduction these penalties cause in the magnitude of the coefficients \bm{\beta}, they are frequently called Shrinkage methods.
Now, the penalty term ends up “measuring the size” of the coefficients. Look at the summary quantities of our variables above. Compare the magnitude of the predictors with the response. The predictors are in different scales. So, if we add some penalties according to the size of the coefficients, we need to put all predictors on the same scale. Otherwise, some variables will be more penalized than others. We do this by standardizing the predictors, a step that must be done in regularization.
Regularization methods help us to mitigate overfitting and, in the case of LASSO, it helps us with variable selection.

Why shrinking \beta regularizes the model?
In the following, we will restrict our focus to LASSO.

title: About
text:
My name is Rodolfo Lourenzutti. I am from a small coastal town in Brazil called Vila Velha (the metropolitan area has approx. 1.7 million inhabitants, but the city alone has approx. 500k inhabitants.) I have a beautiful wife and kid (16 months at the time of writing), and a dog named Git. Git loves fetching stuff (yeah, I named him Git just to make this joke!). He’s an adorable 38 pounds Red Golden Retriever. He’s now three years old.
As a kid, I wanted to do a ton of different things. I wanted to be a doctor, a pilot of a fighter jet, and a psychologist, among other gazillion things. As I grew, I was slowly settling into Computer Science. But then, at the time of application to the university, I suddenly changed my heart and decided to study Statistics (in Brazil, it is different - you pick your major before entering the university, not after). After that, I moved on to do an MSc in Statistics. I was fascinated by the broad range of applications of  Statistics. No matter who I talked to, there was always a data collection and analysis part of their job, and very commonly, I heard the sentence, “You study statistics? Yuck! I had one course in college, very hard!” I found that fascinating. To see how people love their field yet dislike the methods that analyze the data for them to make conclusions that they find very interesting. I have always found it liberating that I could work with people in many different areas. Did you get tired of Business Analytics? Let’s move to biomedicine. Got tired of that too? Let’s move to social-economic studies. Or finance? Or maybe the construction industry? Whatever!
After my MSc, I started my Ph.D. in… care to guess? Statistics! But then, I decided to see things from a different perspective and switched to Computer Sciences. During my Ph.D., I visited the University of Alberta, Dept. of Electrical and Computer Engineering, and worked there for ten months. I returned to Brazil, finished my Ph.D., then moved back to U of A, but this time to the Dept. of Civil Engineering. I worked as a postdoc there for around 18 months. Then, I joined the Master of Data Science (MDS) at UBC as a Teaching Postdoctoral Fellow.
I worked at MDS under the mentorship of Michael Gelbart and Tiffany Timbers, who taught me A LOT about how to teach. After two years, I joined the Statistics Department at UBC in 2020, where I still am. I love teaching, I love data analysis, and I absolutely enjoy interacting with students. They are so interesting!
I created this website to share some of my thoughts and ideas about teaching and some tutorials about statistical methods and data analysis. I hope you find this material helpful. Please let me know if you have any questions or requests for tutorials. You can create an issue here.
